appearance.title: Appearance
appearance.description: Change the visual theme of the application

step1.title: Portal map
step1.body: This screen explains the full flow: define a scenario, the backend prepares it, AKS runs the test, and results are compared with metrics.
details: Start here when you want to understand what each page does before changing parameters.
The portal measures combinations, not brand names: broker, architecture, protocol and format form one unit.
The tutorial button can always be replayed when a new user needs the platform.

step2.title: Catalog before configuration
step2.body: The Catalog explains which components exist, which versions were considered and which combinations make sense before creating a scenario.
details: Open component cards when you are unsure whether a platform fits a protocol.
Compatibility notes prevent tests that are hard to defend later.

step3.title: Scenarios as test contracts
step3.body: In Scenarios you decide the real benchmark composition. Platform, architecture, protocol and format determine the load and how the score should be read.
details: The composition must answer a concrete question: what do you want to compare and under which load?
Data format is not decorative: it changes payload, recommended rate and score context.

step4.title: Executions and cluster state
step4.body: Executions shows every run launched on the cluster. It tells you whether a test is pending, running, completed, failed or stopped.
details: Filter so active tests do not get mixed with historical tests.
Open details when you want live metrics without losing the row context.

step5.title: Results with score explanation
step5.body: Results turns runs into comparisons. Do not stop at the final number: review contributions, errors, throughput and latency.
details: Filters change which scenarios enter the visible calculation.
The score summarizes metrics, but the technical decision comes from the breakdown.

step1.title: Filter the catalog
step1.body: Filters help you quickly find platforms, protocols and architectures. Before creating a test, check that the component exists and is documented.
details: Filter by category when you only need brokers, protocols or architecture patterns.
Search also helps find versions, tags and reproducibility notes.

step2.title: Open a component card
step2.body: Each card summarizes one benchmark component. Opening it shows what it represents, when it makes sense and how it may affect the test.
details: Cards prevent blind component choices.
The short description explains the component role in the message path.

step3.title: Read compatibility
step3.body: The compatibility matrix shows which platforms can work with each architecture and protocol. It is the conceptual filter before the form.
details: A compatible combination is easier to defend in the thesis.
If a combination needs a gateway or adapter, that must be clear before running it.

step4.title: Reproducibility and configuration
step4.body: The detail view separates overview, reproducibility and configuration into tabs. The important part is being able to repeat the test under the same conditions.
details: Review versions, ports, limits and snippets before comparing results.
Copying configuration keeps the benchmark traceable.

step5.title: Send the component to Scenarios
step5.body: When a component fits the test, use it as a starting point to create or adjust a scenario.
details: You do not need to remember the exact component name: the UI can preselect it when possible.
This step connects the catalog with the real benchmark flow.

step1.title: Create a new scenario
step1.body: The New scenario button opens the configuration form. Here you define the test that will later be launched as a cluster run.
details: Give it a name that explains broker, format and test purpose.
A well-named scenario makes Executions and Results readable.

step2.title: Choose the composition carefully
step2.body: Composition is the most important part: platform or broker, architecture and protocol. This combination defines what system you are measuring.
details: Choose the platform first because it filters compatible options.
Architecture and protocol must make sense together: queues, streaming and pub/sub answer different questions.
If the composition is weak, the final score cannot fix the interpretation.

step3.title: Data format and load
step3.body: Data format directly affects payload, recommended rate and result interpretation. IoT, financial, 4K video and 8K video are not equivalent.
details: When you select a format, the UI proposes coherent payload and messages per second.
Format also frames the score: acceptable video latency may not be acceptable for IoT.
If you change payload manually, verify that the broker accepts that size.

step4.title: Predefined scenarios
step4.body: Presets are prepared starting points for common cases. They help you test quickly and understand recommended combinations.
details: Use a preset when you want a reasonable test without building it from scratch.
Then duplicate or edit it to adapt it to a concrete hypothesis.

step5.title: Scenario actions
step5.body: From the list you can execute, duplicate, edit or delete scenarios. Execute creates a run; duplicate is useful when changing only one variable.
details: Duplicate when comparing Kafka against RabbitMQ under the same load.
Edit if the configuration has not been launched yet or if payload, rate or protocol need correction.
Execute only when composition and format already make sense.

step6.title: Sustained mode and warm-up
step6.body: Sustained mode acts like a controlled indefinite mode: stable load, but with limits. Warm-up lets the system settle before measuring.
details: Do not compare cold starts with stable results.
Duration and warm-up must be long enough for defensible numbers.
If a run is too short, the score may reflect start-up noise.

step1.title: Filter executions
step1.body: Dropdowns separate runs by status, scenario, broker, platform, architecture, protocol and format. This avoids comparing unrelated groups.
details: Filter by running when you only want live tests.
Filter by format or broker when validating a concrete hypothesis.

step2.title: Open run details
step2.body: Each row is one concrete execution. Clicking it opens expanded information without losing the full list.
details: The detail view keeps context even when backend updates arrive.
Review status, duration, format and parameters before interpreting metrics.

step3.title: Live metrics
step3.body: When a run is active, the detail panel shows live metrics. They help spot errors, saturation or impossible values early.
details: Stable throughput and low errors indicate the test is progressing well.
High latency or errors may indicate oversized payloads or broker misconfiguration.

step4.title: Stop or delete
step4.body: Row actions let you stop running tests or remove executions you do not want to keep. Stopping preserves partial data when available.
details: Stop a test if it saturates the cluster or the configuration was wrong.
Deleting is destructive for the historical reading of that run.

step5.title: From run to history
step5.body: When a test finishes, persisted measurements feed Results. Executions is operational control; Results is analysis.
details: Do not judge a scenario only while it is running: wait until the run closes and persists data.
History aggregates visible executions of the same scenario.

step1.title: Filter before comparing
step1.body: Results filters decide which scenarios enter the comparison. If you change the visible set, the score context changes too.
details: Compare scenarios with the same format when you want a fair read.
Filter by platform or protocol when you want to isolate one variable.

step2.title: Read key metrics
step2.body: Latency, throughput, errors and measurement volume explain the real behavior. The score only summarizes those data points.
details: Lower latency does not always win if throughput drops or errors appear.
Read percentiles and averages with the data format in mind.

step3.title: Open history detail
step3.body: Clicking a row opens the aggregated scenario detail. It shows which executions contributed and the persisted totals.
details: This detail tells you whether a score comes from one run or several.
Measurements are telemetry points; messages show processed volume.

step4.title: Understand the score
step4.body: The breakdown shows each metric weight, normalized value, contribution and error penalty. That makes the final number auditable.
details: Latency, P50, P99, throughput and errors do not have the same weight.
The penalty prevents a scenario from looking good if it loses or fails messages.
The final score is a guide, not a replacement for metric reading.

step5.title: Compare scenarios side by side
step5.body: The comparison view shows direct differences between two scenarios. It is useful when you changed one variable and want to see the impact.
details: The best comparison changes one variable: broker, protocol, architecture or format.
If you change many things at once, the UI shows numbers but the conclusion is weaker.

home.title: Async APIs
home.subtitle: Real benchmarks under the same load
home.description: This portal compares combinations of broker, protocol, architecture and data format. The idea is simple: run equivalent tests and see with data which design performs best.
home.heroSubtitle: Backstage to create tests, AKS to run them, and metrics to compare results.
home.brokerBody: Stores and redistributes messages independently of producers and consumers

sections.systemDesc: The portal is not the system being measured. Backstage is the interface; the backend prepares the test; AKS runs the components; and metrics enable comparison.
sections.conceptsDesc: A test does not compare brand names: it compares a complete message path under a specific load.
sections.brokerDesc: A message broker decouples producers from consumers: each side works at its own pace without depending on the other.
sections.executionTitle: What happens when you launch a benchmark?
sections.executionDesc: Every run must go through the same steps so the comparison is defensible afterwards.

systemParts.title: Backstage
systemParts.subtitle: Work portal
systemParts.text: The screen where the user creates scenarios, launches executions and reviews results.

systemParts.title: Backend
systemParts.subtitle: Composer
systemParts.text: Applies the thesis rules and converts the scenario into an executable test.

systemParts.title: AKS
systemParts.subtitle: Cluster
systemParts.text: Runs brokers, load generators and metric services in Kubernetes.

systemParts.title: Broker
systemParts.subtitle: Measured system
systemParts.text: Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS or compatible receive the same payloads under load.

systemParts.title: Metrics
systemParts.subtitle: Run data
systemParts.text: Each execution publishes periodic measurements with counters and percentiles.

systemParts.title: Comparison
systemParts.subtitle: Final result
systemParts.text: The UI shows differences between combinations and helps defend conclusions.

concepts.title: Architecture
concepts.text: The way of organising producer, broker and consumer. Affects how the message flows.

concepts.title: Protocol
concepts.text: The communication rules. Decides how the message is sent, acknowledged or delivered.

concepts.title: Data format
concepts.text: The payload type. Sending IoT telemetry is not the same as video or transactions.

concepts.title: Broker
concepts.text: The platform that receives and distributes messages. It is the main piece we compare.

executionSteps.label: Create scenario
executionSteps.sub: choose broker, architecture, protocol and format

executionSteps.label: Prepare execution
executionSteps.sub: the backend applies duration, rate and payload

executionSteps.label: Run on AKS
executionSteps.sub: the generator sends payloads to the broker

executionSteps.label: Save measurements
executionSteps.sub: each run records latency, throughput and errors

executionSteps.label: Compare results
executionSteps.sub: the history lets you see what worked best

pages.label: Catalog
pages.desc: Components, versions and configuration needed to replicate the tests.

pages.label: Scenarios
pages.desc: Define broker, architecture, protocol, format, load, rate and payload.

pages.label: Executions
pages.desc: Launch runs on the cluster, stop tests and review the status of each execution.

pages.label: Results
pages.desc: Compare latency, throughput, errors and percentiles per execution or scenario.

metrics.title: P99 Latency
metrics.text: Measures the time a message takes from leaving the producer to reaching the consumer. The 99th percentile reveals the real worst case: 99% of messages arrive below this value. Low P99 latency is key for real-time systems.

metrics.title: Throughput
metrics.text: Calculates the number of messages processed per second during the test. Indicates the maximum capacity of the broker under the configured load. High throughput with stable latency is the sign of a well-sized system.

metrics.title: Error rate
metrics.text: Shows the percentage of messages that fail, are lost or do not reach the consumer correctly. Includes network errors, timeouts and messages discarded by the broker. A value above 0.1% is usually a warning indicator.

catalog.heading: Component catalog
catalog.subheading: Components, versions and configuration needed to replicate the tests.
catalog.searchPlaceholder: Search by name, version, tag, category or reproducibility
catalog.noComponents: No component found with the current filters.
catalog.tableSubheading: Select a component to see the full card.

categoryDescriptions.architecture: Pattern describing how messages flow within a scenario: via events, queues, logs or streaming.
categoryDescriptions.protocol: Communication rules that specify how messages are sent and received between producer, broker and consumer.
categoryDescriptions.platform: Real broker or platform deployed on the cluster. It is the piece that processes messages and determines results.

categoryImpacts.architecture: Changes the flow shape and can therefore affect latency, decoupling and consumption capacity.
categoryImpacts.protocol: Changes the transport and acknowledgements. Can affect compatibility, errors, latency and message loss.
categoryImpacts.platform: Changes the implementation running on AKS. This is where versions, ports, topology and limits matter for replication.

compatibilityNotes.Kafka: Reference for logs and streaming. When combined with non-Kafka protocols, the scenario must clearly declare the gateway or adapter.
compatibilityNotes.RabbitMQ: Strong in queues, ACKs and flexible routing. The natural platform for AMQP and queue-work tests.
compatibilityNotes.Confluent: Kafka-compatible variant to compare streaming behaviour with a different distribution of the same ecosystem.
compatibilityNotes.NATS Server: Very lightweight for pub/sub and low latency. For large payloads, verify max_payload before launching the benchmark.

modal.noDescriptionLong: This component does not yet have a description in the catalog.
modal.overviewFallback: Component used by the benchmark portal.
modal.impactFallback: Its impact depends on how it is combined inside the scenario.
modal.reproDescription: Version, configuration, and replication steps: only what is needed to reproduce the same conditions.
modal.noReproRows: This component does not yet have structured reproducibility data.
modal.noConfigSnippet: This component does not yet have a configuration snippet.
modal.useDescription: Open the scenario form with this value preselected when possible.
modal.sectionWhat: What it represents within the benchmark

stats.possibleCombinations: possible combinations without applying the matrix

scenarios.heading: Benchmark scenarios
scenarios.subtitle: Load configurations for testing asynchronous API combinations

format.appliedRecommendations: Recommended rate and payload will be applied

warmup.label: Warm-up
warmup.tooltip: Initial unmeasured period so the system can stabilize

modal.natsWarning: Remember to verify max_payload on the NATS broker before sending large payloads.
modal.validationRequired: Name, platform, architecture and protocol are required.

deleteModal.title: Delete Scenario
deleteModal.message: Are you sure you want to delete this scenario? This action cannot be undone.

executeModal.messageConfirm: Are you sure you want to launch an execution for this scenario?
executeModal.messageRunning: The run is being created on the cluster. Please wait.
executeModal.messageDone: The execution was successfully launched on the cluster.
executeModal.messageError: Could not launch the run. Check the configuration or try again.

guide.title: Quick guide
guide.tipSuffix: as a starting point. They are optimized for common use cases and act as references for understanding the recommended combinations.

structure.title: Scenario structure
structure.desc: Each scenario combines a Platform, a messaging Architecture and a transport Protocol. Select them in order: the platform automatically filters compatible architectures and protocols.

parameters.title: Execution parameters
parameters.desc: Duration controls how long the benchmark runs. Rate is the send rate. Payload is the size of each message. Together they determine the total load on the AKS cluster.

sustained.title: Sustained mode
sustained.desc: Sustained mode replaces the old indefinite mode: it is always capped at one hour and applies a recommended rate and payload based on format, platform, architecture and protocol.

formats.title: Data formats
formats.desc: The data format simulates real loads. Controlled baseline: 256 B. 4K video: 500 KB. 8K video: 2 MB. Financial: small JSON messages. IoT: minimal high-frequency telemetry.

guide.tip: Start by selecting the platform to filter compatible options.

table.empty: No scenarios found. Create the first scenario to get started.
table.emptyCreate: Create one with the button above.

rabbitmqFinancialReliable.name: Reliable financial RabbitMQ
rabbitmqFinancialReliable.desc: AMQP queues for short transactions. This is the most defensible preset for observing latency and errors without huge payloads.

natsIotTelemetry.name: NATS IoT telemetry
natsIotTelemetry.desc: Lightweight pub/sub with minimal payload. This is the recommended NATS case before testing heavy loads.

kafka4kStreaming.name: Kafka 4K streaming
kafka4kStreaming.desc: Streaming log with a 500 KB payload. Useful for measuring sustained volume without reaching the 8K limit.

rabbitmqFinancialTransactions.name: RabbitMQ financial transactions
rabbitmqFinancialTransactions.desc: AMQP over RabbitMQ with a compact financial JSON format. It compares latency and errors in short transactions.

confluent8kVideo.name: Confluent 8K video
confluent8kVideo.desc: Heavy scenario for validating payload and throughput limits. Recommended only if the broker accepts 2 MB messages.

kafkaBaselineControl.name: Kafka baseline control
kafkaBaselineControl.desc: Short preset to verify the producer-broker-consumer path before heavier tests.

guide.title: Executions
guide.subtitle: Each run is an execution of a scenario on the AKS cluster.

items.title: Launch a run
items.text: Go to Scenarios and press Execute. The system creates a run and adds it here.

items.title: Follow the progress
items.text: Runs show real-time status: pending, running, completed or error.

items.title: Stop if needed
items.text: You can stop a running run. The run is marked as stopped and partial data is preserved.

items.title: Review results
items.text: Once completed, go to Results to compare metrics between runs and scenarios.

confirm.messageStop: Are you sure you want to stop this execution?
confirm.messageDelete: Are you sure you want to delete this execution? The associated metric data will be lost.
confirm.messageDeleteSelected: Are you sure you want to delete the selected executions?
confirm.messageDeleteAll: Are you sure you want to delete all executions in this group?

glossary.title: How to interpret this page
glossary.heading: Quick glossary
glossary.scoreDescription: The score orders the visible scenarios from best to worst. It combines latency, throughput, errors and resources into a single value to ease comparison.

live.empty: No active executions at this moment.
live.emptySearch: No execution matches the current search.

history.searchPlaceholder: Search by scenario, platform, protocol or format
history.emptyMessage: Run scenarios to see the comparisons here.
history.emptyFilter: No results match the current filters.
history.statsTelemetryVisible: Telemetry points accumulated across visible executions
history.comparisonDesc: Select two scenarios to compare their metrics.
history.tableSubtitle: All executed scenarios with key metrics.
history.tableHint: Click a row to open the expanded scenario detail.

drawer.title: History detail
drawer.subtitle: This summary aggregates the visible executions of the same scenario. Measurements are telemetry points; messages show the processed volume.
drawer.statMeasuresHelper: Persisted points from all visible executions.
drawer.statSentHelper: Total volume sent by the load generator.
drawer.statScoreHelper: Score relative to the visible scenarios under the current filters.
drawer.howToReadBody: Each new execution of this scenario restarts live from zero, but when it finishes it adds its measurements to this scenario's visible history.
drawer.runsDescription: Here you see each individual run for the same scenario. The top-row values are weighted by message count, not a simple average: a run with 10,000 messages carries more weight than one with 500.

score.title: Where the score comes from
score.formula: The score adds each normalized metric by its weight, then applies a penalty if the error rate is too high.
